1.1.label: Question 1.1
1.1.scenario: An individual has a pension policy that includes provision for payment of an ill‐health retirement pension from the date on which she is permanently unable to work.
1.1.question: Discuss whether there may still be a need to purchase a separate IP policy.

Need for separate IP policy: Both policies provide cover for lost income (for example, this might be around two thirds of pre‐disability income in each case) as a result of being permanently unable to do your job as a result of sickness or accident.   [½]
An ill‐health retirement pension will be paid for the rest of your life if you are judged to be permanently unable to do your job between now and your normal retirement age. [½]
An IP policy will pay out if you are either temporarily or permanently unable to do your job, and will only pay benefits until retirement age (or some termination age). [½]
After this, income would come from your pension. [½]
So an IP policy:  complements the ill‐health retirement benefits from a pension policy by providing cover for periods when you are temporarily unable to work    [1]
 duplicates the ill‐health retirement benefits in the period up to retirement (or termination) age because it will also provide benefits if you are permanently unable to work. [1]

1.2.label: Question 1.2
1.2.scenario: Published morbidity tables are based on the combined experience of all the IP insurers contributing to the study.

(i).question: Outline the factors that are likely to give rise to the large differences in the rates observed for different insurers. [3]

Differences in rates between insurers: Published morbidity tables show claim inception and claim termination rates by factors, … [½]
… eg age and deferred period, for policies accepted at normal rates of premium. [½]
Differences in the rates observed for different insurers will arise due to differences in:
 the mix of business by the main rating factors, for example: [½]
– occupation, eg a greater proportion of manual occupations will lead to higher claim rates [½]
 the levels of benefits / terms and conditions offered, which will affect the class of lives attracted to different insurers [½]
 claim definitions, eg ‘unable to undertake one’s own occupation’ [½]
 claims underwriting and subsequent claims management. [½]

(ii).question: Describe the factors that are likely to cause the published tables to become out of date so quickly. [3]

Rates that change rapidly with time: Rates may go out of date very quickly due to:
 changes over time in the opinions about sickness and ability to work, … [½]

… eg at one time any form of psychiatric illness was thought to mean that a person should not work but, with modern drugs and various forms of personal support, opinions have changed and work is now sometimes seen as part of the treatment [½]
 changes in the nature of work, … [½]
… eg a shift from manual jobs to more sedentary jobs is likely to lead to: [½]
– a fall in claim inception rates from accidents [½]
– a rise in claim inception rates from new types of claim, such as repetitive strain injury (RSI) and work‐related stress. [½]
Such changes can occur within a decade and so claim rates can change quickly. [½]

1.3.label: Question 1.3
1.3.scenario: A health and care insurance company sells individual long‐term income protection (IP) policies, mainly to self‐employed individuals.

(i).question: Describe over‐insurance with regard to IP policies, and give examples of how it can arise. [3]

Over-insurance and how it can arise: Over‐insurance occurs on an IP policy when the ratio of post‐claim income to pre‐claim income is inappropriately high. [½]
This ratio is known as the replacement ratio. [½]
 when the policy is first taken out [½]
 through pre‐claim income levels not keeping up with benefits [½]
 due to a reduction in the tax charged on IP claims [½]
 due to receipt of other (non‐disclosed) sources of income while sick. [½]

(ii).question: Explain how over‐insurance can cause problems for the insurer. [3]

How over-insurance can cause problems: A high initial replacement ratio is likely to lead to anti‐selection, as individuals with a greater tendency to fall sick might be tempted to purchase policies that provide higher benefits [½]
Going forwards, if the IP policy provides an income almost equal to or larger than their current salary, policyholders may be tempted to feign sickness so that they get paid without working. [½]
A high replacement ratio at the claim stage might lead to the insurer cutting back benefits, eg to a pre‐specified proportion of the pre‐claim income. [½]
This can result in policyholders being unhappy that their expectations are not being met. [½]
A high replacement ratio during claims is likely to lead to a reduced incentive for the policyholder to return to work. [½]
This includes both genuine (but slightly reluctant) and fraudulent policyholders. [½]
This will result in increased overall claim costs due to a greater number of claims (as more people will remain sick after the deferred period) and claims being in payment for longer. [½]
If the policy conditions allow high replacement ratios, then the insurer will have to investigate claims more thoroughly in order to identify such cases. [½]
If the insurer does not take action to prevent over‐insurance, this will result in premiums having to be increased to uncompetitive levels, or lower profits for the insurer. [½]

(iii).question: Describe ways in which these problems might be mitigated. [4]

Mitigation suggestions: The insurer might have a maximum benefit formula at policy outset that sets the replacement ratio at an appropriate level. [½]
This formula will typically be based on a percentage of pre‐claim income, and make allowance for tax and other sources of income while sick (such as State benefits). [½]
The insurer could strengthen its financial underwriting procedures, particularly on policies where benefit levels are high. [½]
This is to ensure that it has sufficient evidence on policyholder income levels so that it can establish the potential to over‐insure. [½]
It should ensure adequate training for sales staff and checks on the sales process, to make sure that potential policyholders are discouraged from taking out levels of cover that are too high. [½]
The insurer should also incorporate regular financial reviews throughout the policy term to ensure that the benefit level remains appropriate. [½]
Where benefits need to be cut back at the claims stage, premiums may be refunded to policyholders in respect of any ‘unused cover’. [½]
It could increase active measures to encourage claimants to return to work, such as rehabilitation services (even if there is no financial incentive to return to work). [½]
Similarly, incorporate policy features that provide some financial incentive to return to work (eg partial payments on returning to work in a reduced capacity). [½]

(i).question: For income protection insurance, explain what is meant by: (a) benefit limitation (b) linked claims. [4]

Benefit limitation: Benefit payments under an IP policy will be subject to an upper limit, typically a stated percentage of their pre‐incapacity earnings (eg 60%). [½]
Many companies impose more stringent limitations for higher salaries in excess of specified limits, and some have an overall maximum benefit. [½]
When calculating the amount to be paid to the policyholder, many companies deduct any other income that the claimant may receive if benefits are up to the maximum. [½]

The effect on income net of tax is also very relevant as there may be certain circumstances where the benefit permitted by the formula is more than that required to maintain a claimant’s net income at her pre‐disability level. [½]
Linked claims: This relates to IP claims whereby a claimant, having been in receipt of claim payments, recovers and returns to work. [½]

If he or she suffers a recurrence of the same disability within a specified period (known as the linked claims period), he or she will be eligible for immediate claim payments without the imposition of another deferred period. [1]
The standard definition refers to a linked period of six months. [½]

(ii).question: Describe how these two features are used to control the cost of claims of an income protection insurance policy. [3]

Purpose of the two features: Without benefit limitation there is a risk that IP policyholders might receive benefits that exceed their income prior to becoming unable to work. [½]
This means they might ‘profit’ from the occurrence of the insured event, which goes against one of the main principles of insurance. [½]
They would also have a reduced incentive to return to work. [½]

The insurer would like to encourage claimants to return to work because this is good not only for claims costs, but also for the morale of the remaining employees, which may help to prevent further claims. [½]
Benefit limitation also reduces the risk of fraudulent claims. [½]

The existence of the deferred period under an IP policy may deter policyholders from returning to work, since if they fall ill again, then they will have to wait until the end of the deferred period before they start receiving benefits again. [½]
The linked claims period ensures that in the short term they will not have to serve another deferred period, and so encourages policyholders to try working again. [½]

1.5.label: Question 1.5
1.5.question: A health and care insurer is considering the design of an income protection product for people who keep house and do not receive a salary of any kind. Suggest a possible design for this product, focussing on:  the restrictions on the monthly sickness payments that could be imposed  the criteria for the payment of benefits. [7]

Restrictions on benefit: It will met necessary to prevent cases where the policyholder will be better off as a result of claiming rather than being restored to the position they were in before the sickness event. [½]
Imposing a maximum limit on the benefit that can be insured can help with this. [½]
For simplicity, this maximum could be the same for all policies. [½]
The insurer will need to quantify the loss as the result of sickness. [½]
The maximum benefit should be some percentage (<100%) of the cost of hiring labour to do the work that the policyholder can no longer do themselves. [½]
As those who keep house are not in full‐time or part‐time employment, they will be unlikely to receive any State benefits, so there is no contribution to this loss from welfare benefits. [½]
For the effective control of fraudulent claims, there should be a limit on this percentage of, say, 60%. [½]
The payment of benefits (subject to the maximum sum insured) may be contingent on hiring replacement labour so as to reduce the possibility of moral hazard. [½]
There should be a minimum deferred period of a reasonable length (eg 26 weeks) following incapacity, during which no benefit would be paid. [½]
This would help lower costs and reduce the temptation to moral hazard. [½]
It would sensible to have an upper age for benefit payments, eg 60. [½]
Criteria for the benefit payment: Occupational based criteria would not be appropriate, so alternatives must be used. [½]
The stringency of the criteria for benefit payment would influence the premium level.  Detailed rules would cost more to implement, but simple rules might lead to ‘windfall’ claims. [1]
At one extreme, benefits could be paid on any illness or accident that resulted in the policyholder being confined to their house, a hospital or nursing home. [½]

A more stringent approach would be to develop a list of household tasks (eg cooking a meal, stooping to clean), with the benefits being paid if the policyholder was unable to perform a number of these tasks (say 4 out of 6). [1]
As a corollary to this test there could be an overriding condition of total permanent disablement or cognitive impairment. [½]
While benefits are being paid, there will need to be regular checks to ensure that the policyholder still satisfies the conditions for the payment of benefits. [½]

1.6.label: Question 1.6
1.6.scenario: A health and care insurer is proposing to introduce a new income protection insurance policy that incorporates ‘objective cover’. The insurer’s current income protection policies pay claims when the policyholder is unable to perform the material and substantial duties required in their current occupation.  Under the new policy the claims entitlement will be based on objective tests.  In order for the benefit payments to be made, the policyholder’s illness or injury must be identifiable by objective medical testing, such as blood tests, X‐rays or MRI scans.

(i).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this new definition of claims management for the insurer. [5]

Advantages and disadvantages for claims: + The cost of claims processing may be reduced because new eligible rules are straightforward to apply and could be administered by less experienced staff, who will be cheaper to employ. [1]
+ Claims processing will also be quicker because the rules are more straightforward. [½]
+ It may discourage policyholders from making fraudulent claims or the fraudulent exaggeration of claims because the tests are more objective. [½]
This will reduce the overall level of claim payments, the number of claims the insurer has to process and the number that are refused. [½]
Costs may be increased because tests, some of which are expensive (eg MRI scans) will be an extra expense. [½]
A large proportion of claims are for psychiatric illness (eg depression, stress) and pain (eg RSI, back pain), and it may be difficult to find objective tests for these cases. [1]
It may lead to more disputed claims because there are more rules to dispute. [½]
There may be some conditions that legitimately stop people from working, but which are not confirmed by tests. [½]
According to the proposal such people would no longer get benefit, but would still unable to earn a living. [½]
There could be windfall payments to those who fail a test, but who feel capable of working (and may not have been able to claim under the old rules). [½]
For these two reasons (and possibly others) the policy may no longer meet policyholder needs so well, which may lead to unhappy policyholders, disputes, bad publicity, poor sales etc. [½]

(ii).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this new definition for the marketing of income protection policies by the insurer. [3]

Advantages and disadvantages for marketi: + It may increase business if consumers see the new system as more independent and objective, and not dependent on the insurer’s value judgements. [½]
+ It may attract interest from both sales intermediaries and potential customers by virtue of its novelty.  This may lead to increased sales. [½]
+ If expected claims costs (benefits plus expenses) are reduced then this may result in more competitive premiums. [½]
+/– Not easily comparable to competitors’ products, which may either set it apart from competitors’ products or reduce its marketability. [½]
It takes away the emphasis on being able to do your job and rehabilitation into the work force (which is inappropriate for an IP policy). [½]
It no longer precisely covers the risks that consumers face, and so may be less attractive because it does not meet consumer needs as well as the old policy. [½]
It may give the impression that the policy resembles a critical illness policy (with an income benefit) rather than an income protection policy, leading to confusion by consumers. [½]
More marketing may be needed to promote this new type of cover. [½]

1.7.label: Question 1.7
1.7.question: State the four risk factors that are important for income protection business, and explain how each affects the risk.

Important risk factors: age – older lives have a greater number of sickness periods that tend to last longer than those for younger lives [1]
gender – females have higher sickness rates than males [1]

occupation – occupations involving a greater proportion of heavy manual work tend to have higher claim rates than those involving no manual work; the stress associated with an occupation can influence claim rates [1]
area of residence – climate, presence or absence of tropical diseases and ease of access to good medical services can influence claim rates. [1]
Also important are: smoking status, sales channel (business from independent intermediaries tends to have lighter mortality/morbidity). [½]

1.8.label: Question 1.8
1.8.question: Explain the difference from the point of view of the policyholder of buying IP insurance for the next 20 years by:  buying a 20‐year IP policy with premium reviews every five years  buying a series of four 5‐year IP policies each with guaranteed premiums.

Benefits received: Benefits will cease at the end of the term of each policy, so the 5‐year policies will only pay out for a maximum of 5 years (less the deferred period), whereas the 20‐year policy will guarantee cover for the whole period (less the deferred period). [1]
Therefore the 20‐year policy may be more valuable to the policyholder than one where sickness payments will stop at the end of the current 5‐year term. [½]
Premiums paid: Under the 20‐year policy, premiums may change up or down at each review date to reflect changing expected future claims experience for the portfolio of policies [½]
Under the 5‐year policy, premiums for each 5‐year policy will reflect the insurer’s view of the expected claims experience and expenses for the next 5 years [½]
affected by: increase in age, higher initial expenses, changes in profit criteria. [½]
policyholders are subject to underwriting every 5 years, so if their health deteriorates then they may not be accepted at standard rates (or at all) in the future [1]
Under 5‐year, insurer has more flexibility to change premiums/benefits, therefore lower contingency margins needed. [½]
5‐year policy gives more flexibility to change benefit level, deferred period etc to meet changing needs. [½]
Policyholders who lapse early in 20‐year will be better off under 5‐year as they pay lower premiums initially. [½]
